speaker: interviewer
text: January 25, 1996. Survivor-- Kaz Wolff-Zdzienicki. Interview-- Padigail Meskin. City of Byrne Village, Natal. Country-- South Africa. Language-- English. Kaz, would you tell us the story of Bart [INAUDIBLE], please?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I remember a story when I was working on the road close to the crematorium. And on the ditches, I was putting barbed wires along the place. There was a transport on the ramp. They passed the selection and was selected a group of children from two, three, up to 10, 12 years old. They had been surrounded by the SS, mostly SS women, and on, under the charge of very beautiful SS woman, Irma Grese. Was a big, tall, blonde, Valkyria you know, we called her. She was always working with a beautiful shepherd, German shepherd, beautiful dog.

speaker: interviewee
text: And they came before the crematorium, before the entrance to the gas chamber. And they, the children have been ordered to leave their clothes outside. It was surprising, because normal, they went in clothes inside. But sometime, they told them to dress off outside. So they left their dresses outside. And one of the girls had a red ball. And they started playing with the red ball, you know?

speaker: interviewee
text: And at once, Irma Grese whistled and called, children, stop. Finish. We go to take a shower. And the children ran down in steps down to the gas chamber. There was a little boy who was too young to play with the red ball. I swear, he was-- his legs were too short to go the steps down. So the Irma Grese have taken him in hands and had carried him down. And then I saw when the blonde hair of Irma Grese and the black hair of the little boy in her arm who was playing and showing her insignia on a cap disappeared in the entrance of the gas chamber. After a few minutes, Irma Grese came out, walked around, collected the leash of her dog from a SS man who was keeping the dog for the time being. Then I saw the tins of Zyklon B. It was a poison. It was a gravel, green gravel. They put that in the ventilator all around, close the ventilator. The action was completed. We heard the ventilator working after a couple of minutes to give fresh air to the gas chamber. Because now, the Sonderkommando, which I told before, have to walk in, take the corpses, put into the oven, and burn them. And so it was the little, innocent children have been finished. It was a question of couple of minutes. So it was like that. I know that Irma Grese was arrested after, condemned to death. But it was, thanks God, in the English occupation zone. And apparently, the death sentence was executed.

speaker: interviewer
text: Kaz, can you please tell us, were there any resistance activities in the camp? Tell us if there were.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, to a certain extent, yes. Of course, it was very secret, very secret. There have been two kinds of resistance movements. One, I was not involved in. It was collecting and passing information from the camp to the outside world. This way, for example, have been sent outside the camp, photographs taken by Dawid Warszawski from the roof of one of the barracks. He was covering the barracks, you see, with a, the special bitumen paint. And Dawid had done the photographs. He collected-- he find a camera through this, what I told you, Canada, from the warehouse, and used this camera. And these photograph was sent outside.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where did they send the photographs to?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Birkenau. In Birkenau, from the roof of one of the barracks next to the crematorium number four. And they got their people, naked women, before the entrance to the gas chamber. It was publicized all around the world after war. This was one.

speaker: interviewee
text: Second, which I was a little bit involved, was helping the people to escape. You know, what was the procedure? If somebody tried to escape, it was impossible to escape direct from the camp. Because the camp was surrounded with barbed wire under power, electricity. Plus, you had, on the towers, you had machine guns and guards. And during the day, the guards surrounded outside-- not the camp concrete one or two, three, fields of the camp, but all around the area where we worked, all around.

speaker: interviewee
text: Only by night, they come on the towers surrounding different camps. And by day, they have been ready. So it was a question to hide yourself somewhere outside the main camp for three days, because when they find out that somebody escaped, for three days they called this the [? grosse ?] [? postkarten. ?] The big chain of guards was far away from the camp, waiting for the prisoner who escaped, that he will try to pass. After three days, nobody passed. So they moved at night, the guards, close to the camp, direct to the barbed wire. And this prisoner, who already was in hiding outside, was already outside the chain of the guards. So it was much easier to escape. It was question to supply them in advance, in this place where they were-- they're supposed to be in hiding-- with food and water for three days. This was not my job. My job was to take a prisoner who intended to escape, or who was, I would say, was lucky to receive the right to escape. Because not everybody, maybe if they-- not everybody could. You should have [? be or ?] somebody to escape or a chap who, after escaping from the camp, will give some information, contact, and so on. Or was-- had a case which could finish with his death in the camp. It mean his case was not closed. He was on the diff-- this on another suspicion. But we received this another way information that they are closing his case. And him, afterwards, death sentence, will be executed in the camp. So this was a priority to escape.

speaker: interviewee
text: My job was to take to my kommando on which I was working. And when, where really the kapo who was a Czech received from me some food from my parcels and have done this, what I told them. And I have taken one or two people to my kommando for, I would say, recovery, physical rehabilitation, before he goes escaping, you know?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was for about two weeks or three weeks, I had couple of Russian who passed through my hands. I remember them well. Well, one was unlucky. They catched him, killed him. They, there were two. The other one was hanged in the camp. But a couple of them got through to the partisan in the mountains, which were, you know, not far from the camp. It was--

speaker: interviewer
text: So your resistance movement in the camp was linked to the partisan movement outside?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: And it was all part of your underground army that you had joined right at the beginning?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. Yeah. Yeah. You are right.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: That is was.

speaker: interviewer
text: Could you tell us a little bit about cultural activities? Were there any?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, you know, cultural-- we had no theater. We had no operetta. We had an orchestra who was playing marches once we walked to work and we came back from work. But on Sunday, they gave us a chance, sometime-- the orchestra was playing. And they have been playing beautiful melodies from operas, from operettas, from symphonic music, you see? It was a very good orchestra, you see?

speaker: interviewee
text: You know, they are coming to the camp, have been sent people from all around Europe, with instruments even, with violin, with trombones and clarinets and everything. So they had a chance to select the best one. I met. There were people from France, from Poland, from Italy, from Hungary, from operas, from philharmonia, from conservatoriums, and so on and so on, so very high professional, high professional, people, you know?

speaker: interviewee
text: And then we enjoyed sometimes, it was about nine, 10 o'clock in the morning. For two hours, they played. We had-- we forgot completely about the camp, about-- of course, not all, but not all were interested, but most of us. There was, before the kitchen, you know, there was a place of the orchestra close to the gate, entrance gate to the camp. It was a plenty, with, with thousands of people there, sitting and watching and listening to this beautiful. It was, I would say, a visit from the outside world in the death camp. And then another story-- the SS, as you know, the German, are very keen in sport. As we had couple of good boxers in the camp, even European champion, so they organized boxing between two them. Of course, no one had a physical condition as he had before. Even I remember a Polish boxer who was European champion fighting there, you know, with a Hungarian and this and that. It was rather-- And then was in-- before the war, if you maybe remember, a very good team, soccer team, was a Hungarian team. When the Hungarian Jews started to arrive, the, the German organize soccer teams to play. And they played. I remember. It was a-- I, I remember still the name of a, of a referee. It was a Slovak under the name [? Schpitzer. ?] And he was a referee there among the Hungarians, because he spoke a little bit Hungarian. And he was a referee before the war in soccer.

speaker: interviewee
text: So they have been playing. And I remember such a sad picture when-- it was in a behind the hospital camp, there was a field, green field, plenty of SS, because they enjoyed to look on, to watch, a little bit of prisoners from the hospital, couple of doctors, male nurse. And here, the two Hungarian teams playing, and their background were crematoriums, from which went the smoke and fire up. They were playing here, and their families have been just burned in the background. So what a sad picture, yeah? Ah, well. Was there any kind of facilities for art or--

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: Drawing.

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: Or anything like that? Nothing?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, what was art in Auschwitz? Art of survival, how to survive-- this was the art. There was no, no books, no paper, no newspaper, not even German newspaper. It was forbidden, everything.

speaker: interviewer
text: Kaz, can you tell us how you actually-- what happened when you left Auschwitz? And where did you go? What was the process? And why did you leave Auschwitz?

speaker: interviewee
text: You know, from Auschwitz, in October when the East Front approached, they decided to evacuate. And first, they sent the Jews away in different directions, to different concentration other camp, deep in Germany. Eventually, on the 29 of October, '44, they sent us two big transports. The first one was sent to Austria, to a concentration camp in Austria. The second one went when I was, to the concentration camp Sachsenhausen, which was Oranienburg-Sachsenhausen, about 20 kilometers northwest from Berlin. We arrived there. And we were sent to Heinkel. It was a factory of planes, plane factory, Heinkel, to work there. We were at about 4,000. It was small camp there, 4,000. And on the-- was still, the food was disappearing. The bread which was cut in four in Auschwitz now was cut in 12. We had really very poor food. Of course, this was already when the front moved more to the west, the parcels, food parcels, stopped. So we have to rely only on this, what we receive in the camp. So we were hungry day and night. I remember I had a dream in Heinkel. I had a dream that I received from somebody a slice of bread with this beetroots marmalade on top. And I was eating this. And I waked up. I say, what a beautiful dream I had. Well, and on the 13th of April, '45, arrived 700 bombers from American bomber command, and bombed out the factory. From 4,500 people after-- after this bombardment-- daytime, about two o'clock-- they have done a roll call. There were 1,500 alive of prisoners.

speaker: interviewer
text: Why, at this very moment, say, three weeks before the end of the war, they bombed a concentration camp, knowing that there are prisoners inside, knowing that this production there was useless, really, because we never arrived to the front in time? We produced the Dornier planes, you know? But was far behind the schedule and never will be ready for going to the front.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, so it was a disaster there. And then about on the 20, 20, a couple, one week later, we went a roll call. In early morning, we started march, evacuating Sachsenhausen, direction northwest. We passed through the Mecklenburg Forest northwest of Berlin. On the road, we met columns of women. And I was surprised how the women behaved. Among our men groups, people collapsed, break mentally, break physically. If somebody could not work in your row, was put to the side in the ditches and shot by the SS, finished.

speaker: interviewee
text: And the women, when the, when we met two columns, a women column and men column, they shouted to us, keep going. Carry on. Heads up. The war is ending. So they gave us, you know, this push. And of course, we understood that if the women can walk and can keep their head up, why we should break down?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, eventually, we arrived to a river, Crivitz, to a bridge over the river. The SS stopped in this moment. There was a long field around, close to the river, close to the bridge. And the SS started to destroy their personal papers. And I remember, close to me was an SS NCO destroying the paper. And I handed my hand out when I saw that he had pulled out the pistol.

speaker: interviewee
text: I say, give it to me. And he looked at me with stupid face and gave me the pistol. And then, after a couple of minutes, to my surprise, I saw on the bridge a Polish officer in uniform on a horse. I said, what's going on? It was not far from this bridge, was a prisoner of war camp already liberated by the American army. And the Polish officer, with a Polish fantasy, was riding against the German. When he passed the bridge, behind him I saw only the American soldiers and their tanks. And they're hiding in helmets, you know, and going on. And then we came out and, well, we are liberated. This was a liberation moment, you know? And I reported for duty to the army straight away, to the American army. And I was in charge of a platoon after, a platoon. Before we go on there, just, just go back a little bit. When you set out on the march, how many people? Can you remember, were they are a lot of people? Or was it just a small?

speaker: interviewee
text: Lot, lot, thousands.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thousands.

speaker: interviewee
text: Thousands and thousands of people. You know, this was at this very moment, Sachsenhausen was maybe the biggest concentration camp in Germany, because all the camps from the east had been evacuated, you know. And everything came to Sachsenhausen.

speaker: interviewer
text: And when you set off on that journey, did they give you any kind of rations, food, water, or anything like that?

speaker: interviewee
text: We received one-- for the march-- one tin of cold meat for two. And we received half a bread.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how long was that march, about? Can you have any idea?

speaker: interviewee
text: About 10 days.

speaker: interviewer
text: And that's all the rations you received?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: And--

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, you know, when we stopped in the forest for two days, well, I formed a-- they distributed again a small tin of meat. It was for 12. And one bread for 12.

speaker: interviewee
text: So we organized a group of 12. And I told them-- I was more-- more fit, and I don't know. And I was a cook, anyhow. And I told them to collect snails and frogs in the forest, to look for snails and frogs in the forest. And for special-- I told them what kind of bushes they can bring and so on.

speaker: interviewee
text: And we have done a very reasonable soup. It was much better than to eat a little bit of this bread and a little bit of this meat--

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewee
text: --from-- from the-- this canned meat. So we put everything in the water, boiled this. And we had a reasonable soup. And this kept us going for two days.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did many--

speaker: interviewee
text: So it was.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did many people die on that march?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, plenty. Plenty. I don't know. You see, I was, I would say, in the first we was a group of 500, you know, divided into hundreds. [GERMAN], as the Germans say. So it was counted. It was divided. I was in the first group going, so I haven't seen the people killed or dead on the march, because behind me. But there was-- afterwards, I was told by colleagues who came from the group behind-- there were plenty of them. Of course, people have been physically finished. They had no, no condition to, to, to walk. If somebody could not walk, they were shot.

speaker: interviewer
text: And they would just be left?

speaker: interviewee
text: They left. Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: They didn't bury them or anything like that?

speaker: interviewee
text: Nobody [INAUDIBLE] Nobody was interested with, you now.

speaker: interviewer
text: And for that 10 days, there was no stops where you had shelter or anything? You just--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. And after the 10 days, when we arrived, it was a beautiful town. It was, Crivitz was on the border. And then was a beautiful town, German town. And then there were hospitals and this and huge military barracks. We were put inside and then recovered and this. Well, I joined the army. So it was a different story.

speaker: interviewee
text: Some people have make some stupidity, of course. They eat straightaway plenty of food and plenty of fat food and so on and had diarrhea and died afterwards. I, for a couple of days, I ate only rice, you know, and semolina, and things like that, to slowly, slowly-- maybe is I was on medicine before, helped me to not make stupid things, you know? So when you got to the-- to Crivitz.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: You were no longer, you were no longer prisoners now? You were in a barracks, but you weren't prisoners anymore?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. No We are free, completely.

speaker: interviewer
text: So effectively, you had been liberated.

speaker: interviewee
text: No. Then arrived, you see, the Red Cross and the, the Swedish Red Cross to help us. And there were-- there was plenty of food and doctors.

speaker: interviewer
text: And medicine.

speaker: interviewee
text: And everything was normal. Normal life started, and normal life for us, for the group who survived.

speaker: interviewer
text: And can you tell us what that-- do you remember the day? What date is was?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, it was, well, it was the 4th of May.

speaker: interviewer
text: Your birthday.

speaker: interviewee
text: My birthday. It was a, a second gift for my birthday. First was a half a bread in the Auschwitz. And second was my liberation from the concentration camp.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you.
